claims.normalized_claim: Raj Rajaratnam founded and ran the Galleon Group, a family of hedge funds that managed roughly $7 billion at its peak, making him a billionaire and one of the most prominent hedge-fund managers on Wall Street.
evidence_locations: Galleon a hedge-fund family managing roughly $7 billion
Rajaratnam among the wealthiest before the case
allowed_wording: He ran the Galleon Group, a hedge fund that at its peak managed about seven billion dollars.
prohibited_wording: exact peak AUM stated to the dollar as eternal fact
claims.notes: Establishes scale. ~$7B is a peak/approximate figure — keep 'about' / 'roughly'.

claims.normalized_claim: On May 11, 2011, a federal jury in Manhattan convicted Raj Rajaratnam on all 14 counts against him — five counts of conspiracy and nine counts of securities fraud — in what was then the largest hedge-fund insider-trading case of its kind.
evidence_locations: Jury verdict May 11, 2011 — guilty on all 14 counts
allowed_wording: On May 11, 2011, a jury convicted him on all fourteen counts — conspiracy and securities fraud.
prohibited_wording: he admitted guilt (he did not — he was convicted at trial and maintained his innocence)
claims.notes: Convicted at trial = stateable as fact. He did NOT plead guilty; he fought it and lost.

claims.normalized_claim: On October 13, 2011, Rajaratnam was sentenced in Manhattan federal court to 11 years (132 months) in prison.
allowed_wording: He was sentenced to eleven years in prison.
claims.notes: Firm DOJ fact.

claims.normalized_claim: At the time of sentencing, prosecutors and contemporaneous press described the 11-year term as the longest prison sentence ever imposed in a U.S. insider-trading case.
evidence_locations: Prosecutors characterized it as the longest insider-trading sentence imposed
counterevidence: Later cases could match or exceed it; the superlative is time-bound to 2011.
allowed_wording: At the time, prosecutors called it the longest insider-trading sentence in American history.
prohibited_wording: the longest insider-trading sentence ever (stated flatly, undated, as permanent fact)
claims.notes: ALWAYS attribute the superlative to prosecutors/press and time-bind to 2011.

claims.normalized_claim: As part of his criminal sentence, Rajaratnam was ordered to forfeit $53,816,434 and to pay a $10 million fine.
allowed_wording: The court ordered him to forfeit nearly fifty-four million dollars and pay a ten-million-dollar fine.
claims.notes: Use the forfeiture figure ('$53.8M' / 'nearly fifty-four million') as the safest profit-adjacent number.

claims.normalized_claim: In a parallel civil case, the SEC obtained a $92,805,705 penalty against Rajaratnam — described by the SEC as the largest penalty ever assessed against an individual in one of its insider-trading cases.
evidence_locations: Civil penalty of $92,805,705 — largest ever against an individual in an SEC insider-trading case
allowed_wording: The SEC won a record civil penalty of more than ninety-two million dollars.
prohibited_wording: combine the SEC penalty and the criminal forfeiture into one merged number
claims.notes: Keep SEC civil penalty distinct from criminal forfeiture/fine.

claims.normalized_claim: Adding the criminal forfeiture, the criminal fine, and the SEC civil penalty, the combined financial sanctions against Rajaratnam exceeded $156.6 million.
allowed_wording: Taken together, the penalties against him passed a hundred and fifty-six million dollars.
claims.notes: Derived sum of CLM-0005 + CLM-0006. Present as a total, not a single judgment.

claims.normalized_claim: The Galleon case is widely described as the first major insider-trading prosecution built on court-authorized wiretaps — a surveillance tool previously reserved largely for organized-crime and drug investigations.
evidence_locations: First use of court-authorized wiretaps as the centerpiece of a major insider-trading case
Tool historically used against the mob and drug rings
counterevidence: 'First' is a widely repeated characterization; phrase as 'widely described as' rather than absolute.
allowed_wording: It was the first big insider-trading case built on wiretaps — the same tool the government used on the mob.
prohibited_wording: state 'the very first time wiretaps were ever used in any securities matter' as absolute
claims.notes: Cinematic core. Keep 'widely described as / according to the FBI and prosecutors'.

claims.normalized_claim: In 2013, the U.S. Court of Appeals for the Second Circuit affirmed Rajaratnam's conviction and upheld the wiretaps as lawfully authorized and the recordings as admissible evidence.
evidence_locations: Wiretap authorization upheld as lawful; evidence admissible
allowed_wording: In 2013, the appeals court upheld the wiretaps as lawful and affirmed his conviction.
claims.notes: Primary legal authority. Pays off the legitimacy question about the method.

claims.normalized_claim: Rajaratnam's sources of inside information included corporate insiders charged separately: Rajiv Goel of Intel, Anil Kumar of McKinsey, Robert Moffat of IBM, and Danielle Chiesi of New Castle Funds.
allowed_wording: His sources sat inside the companies themselves — an Intel executive, a McKinsey partner, an IBM senior executive, a fund manager named Danielle Chiesi — each charged separately.
prohibited_wording: merge any tipper's outcome with Rajaratnam's penalties
name a tipper as convicted without checking each individual disposition
claims.notes: Charged SEPARATELY. Goel and Kumar cooperated with the government; keep individual roles precise and attributed.

claims.normalized_claim: Rajat Gupta — a former Goldman Sachs director and former global head of McKinsey — was a separate defendant, convicted in June 2012 of one conspiracy count and three securities-fraud counts for tipping Rajaratnam, and was sentenced to two years in prison and a $5 million fine.
evidence_locations: Gupta former Goldman director / ex-McKinsey head
allowed_wording: A separate trial convicted Rajat Gupta — a Goldman Sachs board member — of feeding Rajaratnam secrets from the boardroom; he got two years.
prohibited_wording: Gupta and Rajaratnam were tried together (they were not)
attribute Gupta's penalties to Rajaratnam or vice versa
claims.notes: STRICT separation. Use Gupta only to show the network reached the very top — never merged numbers.

claims.normalized_claim: Estimates of Rajaratnam's illicit gains vary by scope (the SEC cited figures from over $25 million to over $52 million; broader-probe figures ran higher; prosecutors said over $60 million), so the safest single on-screen figure is the court-ordered forfeiture of about $53.8 million, or 'more than $50 million.'
counterevidence: Figures legitimately differ by methodology; no single number is canonical.
allowed_wording: The exact profit depends on how you count it — but the court made him give back more than fifty million dollars.
prohibited_wording: a single flat profit number presented as the definitive amount
claims.notes: Always hedge the profit figure; anchor to the forfeiture.

claims.normalized_claim: The case became public on October 16, 2009, when Rajaratnam was arrested and the SEC and federal prosecutors announced charges against him and several alleged tippers.
allowed_wording: On the morning of October 16, 2009, agents arrested him at his home.
claims.notes: The arrest morning. 2009 = allegations; the 2011 verdict converts to fact.

claims.normalized_claim: The wider Galleon investigation reached far beyond Rajaratnam: the SEC brought civil charges against 29 individuals and entities, and the broader probe produced dozens of convictions and guilty pleas.
evidence_locations: SEC charges against 29 individuals and entities
dozens of convictions across the probe
counterevidence: Tallies vary slightly across sources/time; attribute to SEC/press counts.
allowed_wording: The case was bigger than one man: regulators charged twenty-nine people and firms, and the wider sweep ended in dozens of convictions.
claims.notes: Scope beat. Keep numbers attributed.

claims.normalized_claim: Rajaratnam served the bulk of his sentence and, in 2019, was released from prison to home confinement under the federal First Step Act.
allowed_wording: He served most of his term and, in 2019, was released to home confinement.
claims.notes: Aftermath.

claims.normalized_claim: Rajaratnam later published a memoir (reported as 'Uneven Justice,' around 2021) in which he disputes the case against him and frames his prosecution as unjust.
counterevidence: His account is self-interested and is not evidence of innocence; the conviction stands and was affirmed on appeal.
allowed_wording: In a memoir, he still insists he was wronged.
prohibited_wording: treat his memoir as a neutral fact source or as evidence the verdict was wrong
claims.notes: VERIFY title/date/publisher before air. Use only as 'he says', to be fair, not to relitigate the verdict.

claims.normalized_claim: The jury found that Rajaratnam traded on material, non-public information — corporate secrets passed to him before they were public — about companies including major technology and finance firms, turning advance knowledge into trading profits.
evidence_locations: Convicted of trading on inside information ahead of announcements
allowed_wording: The jury found he traded on secrets — information he wasn't supposed to have, before anyone else did.
prohibited_wording: describe specific trades not in the record as settled fact
claims.notes: The 'edge'. Keep it general; the verdict supports the pattern.

claims.normalized_claim: At trial, prosecutors played the jury recordings of Rajaratnam's own wiretapped phone calls, so jurors heard the alleged scheme described in the defendants' own voices.
evidence_locations: Wiretapped phone calls played for the jury
Agents recorded the defendants' own conversations
allowed_wording: Prosecutors played the jury his own phone calls. The crime, in his own voice.
prohibited_wording: invent or quote specific wiretap dialogue verbatim without verifying it against the trial record
claims.notes: The payoff device. Any verbatim call quote MUST be verified against the public trial record before air.

claims.normalized_claim: Investigators built toward the wiretap by first working with cooperating witnesses and informants; according to news reconstructions, a cooperating witness helped establish the basis for a court-authorized tap on Rajaratnam's phone.
evidence_locations: A cooperating witness helped establish the basis for the wiretap
Escalation from informants to a court-authorized phone tap
counterevidence: Procedural details come from reporting; keep attributed.
allowed_wording: It started, as these things do, with a cooperator — and then they asked a judge for something new: permission to listen.
prohibited_wording: state contested procedural specifics as undisputed fact
claims.notes: Bridge from suspicion to the wire. Attribute to reporting.

claims.normalized_claim: After the charges, the Galleon Group unwound and shut down, and Rajaratnam's career as a hedge-fund manager ended.
evidence_locations: Galleon wound down after the charges
allowed_wording: Within days, the seven-billion-dollar fund began to close down.
claims.notes: The fall of the empire.

findings: 20 claims; all critical claims (CLM-0001/0002/0008/0009/0017/0018) graded A or B with official DOJ/SEC/court sources.

R2 discipline: conviction at jury trial stated as fact (CLM-0002/0003); conduct framed as 'the jury found / prosecutors proved' (CLM-0017); superlative attributed and time-bound (CLM-0004); profit hedged and anchored to forfeiture (CLM-0012).
Co-defendant separation enforced: Gupta is a distinct case with distinct penalties (CLM-0011); tippers charged separately (CLM-0010) — prohibited_wording bars merging.

Memoir (CLM-0016) is grade C / conditional — usable only as attributed opinion, verify before air. Any verbatim wiretap dialogue (CLM-0018) must be checked against the public trial record before locking narration.
